title: Apple and Blackberry Crumble
subtitle: Bramley apple and blackberries under a golden oat crumble topping
excerpt: Bramley apple and fresh blackberries cooked together under the same butter, oat, and demerara crumble topping as the plain apple version. The blackberries add colour, a slight sharpness, and juice that soaks into the underside of the crumble as it bakes.
sourceNotes: Apple and blackberry crumble is a seasonal variation on the plain apple crumble, timed to the overlap between the Bramley apple season (September onwards) and the wild blackberry season (August to October). It is one of the most familiar British autumn desserts.

content.text: Blackberries and Bramley apples are a classic British pair. The apple gives bulk and sweetness once cooked. The blackberries bring colour and a sharp note. Together they make a lot of juice as they bake. The juice bubbles up at the edges and soaks into the underside of the crumble. That leaves a layer that is part crisp and part soft.

Wild blackberries picked from hedgerows in August and September have more flavour than shop-bought ones, though both work. If the blackberries are very tart, add a little more sugar to the filling. If they are very ripe and sweet, reduce the sugar slightly.

items.name: Bramley apple
items.prepNote: peeled, cored, and cut into 3 cm pieces (about 3 large apples)

content.text: Heat the oven to 190°C fan. Combine {{apple-bramley}} of prepared Bramley apple with {{blackberries}} of blackberries and {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar in a 1.5-litre baking dish. Toss gently, don't break the blackberries. Spread level.

Combine {{plain-flour}} of plain flour, {{rolled-oats}} of rolled oats, and {{demerara-sugar}} of demerara sugar in a large bowl. Add {{unsalted-butter}} of cold butter cubes and rub in with your fingertips until the mixture resembles coarse, uneven crumbs with some pea-sized lumps. Do not overwork it.

Scatter the crumble evenly over the fruit. Do not press it down. Bake for 35, 40 minutes until the topping is golden and the fruit juices are bubbling at the edges. The filling will be dark purple beneath the crumble. Rest for 5 minutes and serve with custard or cream.

items.fix: Toss the filling with 1 tbsp of plain flour before adding to the dish. The flour thickens the juices as they bubble. This is more of a consideration with very ripe blackberries than with firmer ones.
items.cause: Blackberries released a lot of juice during baking

items.fix: Raise to 200°C fan for the final 10 minutes. The demerara sugar should be clearly visible in the topping, it gives colour and crunch.
items.cause: Oven temperature too low, or topping was too thick

items.fix: Add an extra 20 g of caster sugar to the filling. Taste a berry before starting, if it makes your mouth dry, the filling needs more sugar.
items.cause: Blackberries were underripe or very tart
items.symptom: Blackberries have turned the filling very bitter

items.fix: Cut the apple into 3 cm pieces. If the crumble is golden but the apple still firm, cover loosely with foil and bake for a further 10 minutes.
items.cause: Cut too thick, or oven temperature too low
items.symptom: Apple is still firm after baking

content.text: Apple and blackberry crumble belongs to the British autumn. Bramley apples come in from September. Wild blackberries run from August into October. Hedgerow blackberries and orchard apples were cooked together in British country kitchens long before the crumble topping became standard. The two fruits had already appeared together in pies and fools well before the wartime years made crumble a staple.
